title: Cloud Drain
shortDescription: Crypto Mining via Stolen Cloud Credentials

briefing.incidentSummary:
A startup received an AWS bill of ₹14.2 lakh for a single month — 60x their usual spend.

Someone had spun up 47 high-performance GPU instances in three regions and was running cryptocurrency mining software around the clock.

The AWS root credentials were leaked. But by who — and how?
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Trace how the credentials were leaked

› Identify who accessed and misused them

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Ketan S
suspects.profileNotes: Pushed AWS root credentials to a public GitHub repo 3 months ago inside a .env file. Instead of immediately rotating the credentials, he quietly removed the commit. A bot scraped the credentials within 8 minutes of the push. Ketan then used the still-valid credentials himself to mine crypto from home.

suspects.name: Automated Credential Bot
suspects.profileNotes: Scraped the credentials within 8 minutes of the GitHub push. However the mining activity began 11 days later — suggesting the human actor (Ketan S) used the credentials himself rather than the bot operator.

suspects.name: Prerna A
suspects.profileNotes: Had legitimate AWS access but only to staging environments. No GPU instance provisioning rights. No activity outside business hours.

suspects.name: Aman V
suspects.profileNotes: Internship ended 5 months ago. AWS access revoked on last day. No activity under his credentials since then.

items.label: oops pushed .env with keys, removing now
items.detail: Git commit message from 3 months ago. Ketan pushed AWS_ACCESS_KEY and AWS_SECRET_KEY in a .env file then immediately deleted the commit — but GitHub history retains the data, and a scraper had already captured it within 8 minutes.

items.label: Why is our AWS bill ₹14 lakh?? Who authorized GPU instances??
items.detail: Prerna's message when the bill alert fired. She immediately raised the alarm and began the investigation. Not involved in the breach.

items.label:
user_data: #!/bin/bash
apt install -y xmrig
xmrig --config /tmp/pool.conf
items.detail: User data script embedded in all 47 GPU instance launches. Installs XMRig — a Monero mining tool — immediately on boot. The pool config points to a mining pool wallet traced to Ketan S.

items.label: aman left 5 months ago but he used to handle AWS stuff. maybe check him?
items.sender: Prerna A (internal Slack, to security team)
items.detail: Prerna A flagged Aman V, a former intern, as a potential suspect during the initial investigation. Aman did handle AWS configurations during his internship. However, his IAM access was fully revoked on his last day — confirmed by the IAM policy audit log. The breach used root credentials from the .env file, not IAM keys. Aman never had access to root credentials.
items.irrelevantReason: Aman V's access was revoked on departure and he never had root credentials. The breach used the root access key committed to GitHub by Ketan S.

items.label: aws_cloudtrail_log.json
items.detail: AWS CloudTrail event log covering the 4-hour mining deployment window. First API call at 02:11:33 AM: DescribeRegions — a standard enumeration call made before multi-region deployment. This is followed by 47 RunInstances calls between 02:14 AM and 02:18 AM, spread across us-east-1 (15 instances), eu-west-1 (16 instances), and ap-south-1 (16 instances). All calls use the root account access key — not an IAM role — confirming the leaked .env credentials were used directly. Each instance was launched with a user-data script that installs XMRig on boot. The EC2 instance type p3.8xlarge costs $12.24/hour per instance — 47 instances running for 30 days produces a bill of approximately ₹14.2 lakh at current exchange rates, matching the actual bill received. Post-launch calls show TerminateInstances was never called — the instances ran until AWS automatically suspended the account for billing threshold breach.

items.label: github_secret_scan_report.txt
items.detail: GitHub secret scanning incident report for repository ketan-sharma-dev/startup-backend. At 11:14:07 PM, commit hash a3f9b21 was pushed containing a .env file with AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY in plaintext. GitHub's automated secret scanner detected the credentials at 11:23:44 PM — 9 minutes and 37 seconds after the push — and sent an email alert to the repository owner. Ketan had already force-deleted the commit at 11:15:02 PM — 48 seconds after the push. However, git history is immutable on GitHub's servers until a DMCA-level purge is requested; the commit remained accessible via the reflog endpoint for 24 hours. An automated credential harvester operated by a threat intelligence firm scraped the commit at 11:22:51 PM — 51 seconds before GitHub's own scanner fired. The AWS credentials were valid and unrestricted. Ketan never rotated them, never filed a GitHub security incident report, and never contacted AWS to invalidate the key.

items.label: mining_wallet_analysis.pdf
items.detail: Blockchain forensics report on the XMRig mining pool wallet used across all 47 GPU instances. The wallet address bc1q...7x4z received 0.847 XMR (approximately ₹1.8 lakh at the time) over the 30-day mining window. Monero's privacy features obscure sender and receiver by default, but the wallet's payout address was configured to auto-forward earnings to a centralised exchange (WazirX) for INR conversion. The exchange's KYC records — obtained via law enforcement request — identify the account owner as Ketan Sharma, PAN card matching Ketan S's employment record. The exchange account was created 3 days before the mining campaign launched, suggesting the infrastructure was planned in advance. Two withdrawal transactions totalling ₹1.76 lakh were made to a UPI account linked to Ketan S's registered mobile number.

hiddenItem.label: vpn_exit_node_trace.txt
hiddenItem.detail: VPN subscriber records obtained via law enforcement subpoena to a commercial VPN provider. The exit node IP 103.55.77.12 — from which all AWS API calls were made — is operated by this provider. Subscriber records for the account assigned this exit node show the username ketan.sharma.dev@gmail.com, matching Ketan S's personal developer email confirmed by colleagues. The account was activated 2 days before the mining campaign. Payment was made using a prepaid card, but the card was purchased via a UPI transaction from Ketan S's registered bank account. The VPN session covering the AWS API calls was initiated from the subscriber's home broadband IP — 115.240.88.91 — which is registered to a residential BSNL account in Ketan S's name at his known address in Bengaluru. The VPN was intended to mask the origin but the subscription trail leads directly back to him.

minigame.title: Trace the AWS API Origin
minigame.hint: Find the VPN exit node used to make the fraudulent AWS API calls.

onSuccess.message: VPN exit node identified. Subscriber record is now unlocked.

items.label: Leak to Launch Timeline
items.detail: Credentials leaked via GitHub at 11:14 PM. Bot scraped them at 11:22 PM. Mining activity began 11 days later at 2:00 AM — suggesting a human who knew the credentials were valid used them deliberately, not the bot.

items.label: GPU Instance Spread
items.detail: 47 p3.8xlarge instances across 3 AWS regions — designed to stay under each region's default vCPU limit to avoid triggering automatic AWS abuse detection. Shows awareness of AWS internal thresholds — someone with cloud knowledge.

items.label: AWS API Call Origin
items.detail: All 47 RunInstances API calls originated from IP 103.55.77.12 — a commercial VPN exit node. The same IP was used for all subsequent instance management calls. VPN subscriber records link it to Ketan S.

timeline.title: Credentials Pushed to GitHub
timeline.description: Ketan S commits .env file with AWS root keys

timeline.title: Bot Scrapes Credentials
timeline.description: Automated scraper captures keys 8 minutes after push

timeline.title: Commit Deleted
timeline.description: Ketan removes the commit — but never rotates the keys

timeline.title: Mining Campaign Launched
timeline.description: 47 GPU instances spun up across 3 AWS regions

timeline.title: Bill Alert Fires
timeline.description: AWS sends ₹14.2 lakh billing alert to startup

timeline.title: CloudTrail Pulled
timeline.description: API logs reveal root credential abuse from VPN IP

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Ketan S exposed as the cloud mining fraudster.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Ketan S.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...₹14 lakh. In one month. For 47 machines nobody authorised.
The keys were out there. Someone knew — and used them.
Find who knew.
aria.exploreFeeds:
CloudTrail logs. GitHub history. Blockchain traces.
Cloud crimes leave receipts. Read them.
aria.viewEvidence:
The keys were never rotated. 11 days passed before launch.
This wasn't a random bot. This was deliberate.
aria.decryptionHint:
The VPN account has a name behind it.
Decode the subscriber username.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
